coredata.dc:title: Palladium-platinum nanostructures from fenugreek extract for Suzuki coupling reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Bathula, Chinna
Mallikarjuna, K.
Cuddapah, Dhananjaya Rao
Chidambara Kumar, K.N.
Kim, Hyun-Seok
Bai, Cheolho
Neelakanta Reddy, I.
coredata.dc:description: To overcome the use of toxic chemicals and harsh experimental conditions for the preparation of nanomaterials, there is a need to develop environmentally friendly experimental protocols by employing the biopolymers with metal nanostructures. To address this issue the polysaccharide covered palladium-platinum (PdPt) nanostructure are prepared with fenugreek seed extract. The phase purity and crystallinity of prepared PdPt nanostructure were analysed through the X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies. The morphological features were determined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. The SEM analysis indicated that the size of the particles is in the order of 2–5 µm and pores of 100–200 nm are also observed. The chemical composition and oxidation states of prepared structures were investigated through the x-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis. As a proof-of-concept the catalytic behaviour of PdPt structures were studied through Suzuki coupling reaction to obtain biaryl compounds (yield 94 %). Hence, the reported sustainable protocol can be employed in the preparation of industrially important catalytic system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polysaccharides
PdPt nanostructure
Suzuki reactions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction analysis of PdPt nanostructure.
Scanning electron microscopic images(a-d), Elemental mapping (e) palladium (f) platinum (g) carbon elements of PdPt nanostructure.
XPS spectra of PdPt nanostructure (a) survey scan and deconvolution spectra (b) C1s (c) N1s (d) O1s (e) Pd 3d and (f) Pt 4f.
(a) Suzuki Coupling reaction, (b) catalyst optimization, (c) Recycling of PdPt nanostructure.
Preparation of PdPt nanostructures from Fenugreek seed extract.
Summary of PdPt catalyzed Suzuki coupling reaction.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 1 mmol boronic acid, 1 mmol of halide, 4 mmol PdPt catalyst, 2 M K2CO3 (5 mL), PEG-400 (10 mL).

head.ce:title: Palladium-platinum nanostructures from fenugreek extract for Suzuki coupling reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome the use of toxic chemicals and harsh experimental conditions for the preparation of nanomaterials, there is a need to develop environmentally friendly experimental protocols by employing the biopolymers with metal nanostructures. To address this issue the polysaccharide covered palladium-platinum (PdPt) nanostructure are prepared with fenugreek seed extract. The phase purity and crystallinity of prepared PdPt nanostructure were analysed through the X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies. The morphological features were determined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. The SEM analysis indicated that the size of the particles is in the order of 2–5 µm and pores of 100–200 nm are also observed. The chemical composition and oxidation states of prepared structures were investigated through the x-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis. As a proof-of-concept the catalytic behaviour of PdPt structures were studied through Suzuki coupling reaction to obtain biaryl compounds (yield 94 %). Hence, the reported sustainable protocol can be employed in the preparation of industrially important catalytic system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bimetallic nanostructures have emerged as an innovative class of environmentally benign porous nanomaterials with various applications [1,2]. They are potential candidates for numerous modern nanotechnologies and organic coupling reactions. Owing to their simple one-pot synthesis, unique excellent photochemical stability, and high catalytic activity, these nanoarchitectures are now on the verge of becoming an alternative source to conventional unit-metal catalysts and inorganic fluorescent complexes [3]. To date, most of the heterogeneous catalytic activities depend on metal catalytic agents for the fabrication of value-added chemical products in energy and the monitoring of environmental applications [4]. However, challenged with the severe energy crisis and environmental deterioration matters, the concerns regarding using the metal-based catalysts are still of extreme worry to the scientists and engineers. Primarily, while much advancement has been made to exploit the employment of prevalent metals, the extensive use of metals, principally precious metals as catalysts, are constrained by the high cost and low abundance in environment. The probable leaching of metal species can result in deprivation of reaction operation, damage of active sites, infection of the products and distribution of toxic pollutants. Therefore, it is highly attractive to find appropriate substitutions to metal-based catalysts, among which Pd materials have arisen as hopeful contenders due to their irresistible characteristic rewards of abundance, resourcefulness, and corrosion resistance [5].

The conventional preparation processes of palladium materials are usually connected with the use of expensive precursors and/or harsh synthetic conditions. Therefore, this strategy can be more appealing and competitive, with the catalysts derived from renewable biomass which is largely available. Interestingly this catalyst system is fully compatible with the principles of green chemistry. Based on these concepts, various biomass materials containing polysaccharides have been employed as the low-cost development of palladium catalysts for diverse potential applications [6–9] and quantum dots on CNT in electrocatalysis [10].

Recently, the design of metallic/bimetallic composites have gained significant attention due to their wide range of applications in catalysis, medicine, optoelectrical, energy conversion and storage [11,12]. Particularly, the interest in investigating plants, and other biological sources for preparation of nanoparticles is increased largely [13,14]. Natural biopolymers like polysaccharides exhibit different structural density and are rich sources of various techno-functional agents which are prepared through facile green synthetic approach [15–17]. The metal nanoparticles possess physicochemical properties inducing synergetic effect between biopolymer and metal core for catalytic process in industrial applications [18]. The metal/metal oxide (silver, gold, palladium, iron, copper and platinum/zinc oxide, iron oxide, titanium oxide and nickel oxide) embedded polysaccharide structures were employed as efficient catalyst for hydrogenation where the polysaccharides also act as reducing and stabilizing agents [19,20]. The naturally occurring polysaccharides are used in the controlled growth of shape and size of the nanoparticles [21,22]. Generally, size and shape of metal particles owing to their high surface to volume ratio and high chemical stability, high surface area, and reusability play a propound role in catalysis [23,24].

Conversely, the monometallic nanoparticles have limitations in large scale applications owing to their gradual degradation of catalytic efficiency and other poison effect in reusability [25,26]. To address these issues, researchers dedicated their efforts towards multi, or bimetallic nanoparticles as alternative materials to enhance the catalytic performance in chemical reactions [27–31]. Nanomaterial based catalysts have been widely used for coupling reactions [32,33]. In-situ biogenesis of Pd on Artemisia extract-altered graphene oxide as catalyst for Suzuki reaction is carried out [34]. These results encouraged us to carry out the catalytic performance of palladium-based materials for coupling and reduction reaction by bimetallic composite [35,36]. Palladium based nanoparticles (PdNPs) can be prepared by various procedures by reducing the metal salt solution using reduction catalysts such as dimethyl formamide [37]. The major concern for using those chemicals will have impact on environmental hazards or toxicity [38]. The green synthetic protocols using polysaccharides will avoid the negative impact during synthesis of PdNPs [39]. The plants parts such as seeds, leaves, roots are reported to be excellent candidates in synthesis of Pd Nps [40].

In this manuscript, we demonstrate a facile one-pot green synthesis of biocompatible PdPt nanostructures from aqueous fenugreek seed extract, their characterization, photophysical properties and their potential application as a catalytic application are studied. Fenugreek is one of the most abundant plants, which comprises of small seed covered with a shell. The key features of using biomass source were simple preparative strategy, environmental friendliness and low cost which led to increase of surface area, control of pore size, lowering of toxicity, improved chemical stability and existence of porous surface. The morphology features like size and shape of the prepared materials analyzed through the scanning and transmission electron microscopy. The crystalline signature was determined with x-ray diffraction analyses and surface features and chemical composition with oxidation states of elements analysed with x-ray photoelectron spectroscopic analyses. Employing PdPt catalyst in Suzuki coupling reaction resulted in the formation of bis aryl derivative. The prepared microscopic structures can be employed for large scale industrial applications.

Platinum chloride (PdCl2, MW = 177.23), palladium chloride (PtCl2, MW = 265.98), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, MW = 40), were procured from sigma-Aldrich and all chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. The fenugreek seeds were obtained from Hubli, Karnataka, India. DI water was used for the experimental process.

Fenugreek seed-based polysaccharides were derived from fenugreek seeds according to previous literature [24]. Fenugreek seed powder (10 g) was soaked in 500 mL deionized water for 12 h. Then obtained dispersion is heated to 100 °C for 90 min and cooled to room temperature. Furthermore, the extract was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 6 min. Thus, formed extract is kept in refrigerator to settle out undissolved materials and decant the upper clear solution. The extract was then filtered through filter paper. 5 mL of the solution was added to 50 mL of an aqueous solution containing 1 mM platinum chloride and 1 mM palladium chloride and adjust the pH solution 10 by using 0.1 N NaOH solution. To accomplish the formation of nanoparticles the reaction mixture was stirred for 24 h at 90 °C. The color of the reaction mixture turns into to dark solution implied the creation of PdPt nano catalyst. The prepared nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation for 20 min at 10,000 rpm. The PdPt material was then washed with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the PdPt nano catalyst was dried and utilized for further characterization and catalytic application. The schematic presentation for the preparation of PdPt structures is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The structural and morphological features of prepared PdPt nanostructure is analysed through different analytical instrumentation techniques. The phase purity and crystalline nature of materials analysed through the X-ray diffraction (PANalyical X’pert Pro) coupled with CuKα1 radiation of 1.54 Å (Thermo fisher Scientific Korea co) at a scan rate of 2°/min within the range 10- 90°. The Morphological features like size, shape and elemental distribution of the prepared materials determined with scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi S-4800) coupled with energy dispersive x-ray analyses (EDX). To understand the morphology, the prepared material is analysed with transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, H-7600, Hitache Inc., Japan). The surface properties such as chemical composition and oxidation states of elements were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Versaprobe II, ULVAC-PHI, Japan) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. The catalytic performance was determined by Suzuki-coupling reaction.

The structural features like crystallinity and the orientation of the planes were investigated through X-ray diffraction analysis, and obtained results are depicted Fig. 1
. The diffracted peaks observed at 40.15°, 46.6°, 68.1°, 81.9° and 86.5° are attributed to the facets of (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes and are well matched with peaks positions described in standard JCPDS data of palladium (JCPDS:05-0681). The diffracted peaks were slightly shifted towards the platinum. Additionally, the platinum and palladium elements are similar group of materials, and their phases are nearly analogous. The average crystallite size of the polysaccharide assisted PdPt structures were estimated through the Debye-Scherrer formula, and it was found to be 27.2 nm. No additional peaks were observed in the XRD data indicating the phase purty of the PdPt structures prepared by Fenugreek seed exact containing polysaccharides by green synthetic protocol.

The morphological features of the prepared polysaccharide assisted PdPt nanostructure were analysed with scanning and transmission electron microscopic studies. The nature of micro particles was clearly observed in SEM analyses and the acquired results were presented in Fig. 2 . The obtained particles are with the size in the order of 2–5 µm as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) and it has the number of pores with the size of 100–200 nm (Fig. 2 (c)) as illustrated through the SEM analyses. The morphological structure like size and shape of the particles strongly influence the properties like catalytic, energy storage, energy conversion, and biological applications. Additionally, the nature of the materials also plays a significant role in catalytic application due to its high surface to volume ratio, induces the activation of more surface atoms which enables the rate of reaction. Furthermore, the performance of the materials not only depend on morphological features but there are also influencing factors such as supporting/stabilizing/surrounded materials with interaction between metals and its supporting materials. The elemental distribution of prepared materials was analysed through elemental mapping analyses and obtained results are depicted in Fig. 2 (d-f). The palladium and platinum and carbon elements were uniformly distributed throughout the nanostructure as shown in Fig. 2 (e-g), determined by EDS elemental mapping studies. The TEM analyses were also performed to have a better understanding of the morphology of prepared PdPt nanostructure, and corresponding results are shown in Fig. 3 . The PdPt particles are in nanometer size and are shielded by biopolymers of polysaccharides (Fig. 3 a) and the pores of the prepared nanostructure certainly visible as presented in Fig. 3 b -c. In addition to that, when carefully observed the polysaccharide layer and metal nanoparticles were easily distinguishable.

The surface chemical composition and oxidation states of polysaccharide assisted PdPt nanostructure were determined with XPS analyses, and attained results are depicted in Fig. 4 . The survey scan spectra of prepared materials indicated the presence of palladium, platinum, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in PdPt covered with polysaccharide (Fig. 4 (a)). The deconvoluted spectra of C1s contains 3 peaks at 284.0, 285.6 and 287.5 eV, which are attributed to the C–C/C–H, C-N and O-C = O or HN-C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4 (b)). The enlarged representative spectra of N1s contains two peaks at 399.2 and 401.3 eV, which are assigned to pyrrolic like nitrogen, and N–H stretching bonds (Fig. 4 (c)). The deconvoluted spectra of O1s contains two peaks at 531.6 and 533.8 eV were assigned to the N-C = O and N-O bonds ( Fig. 4 (d)). The enlarged spectra of Pd 3d element Fig. 4 (e)) reveals two peaks at binding energy 337.3 and 342.5 eV which were attributed to the Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2. The high-resolution spectra of Pt 4f element (Fig. 4 (f)) presented the peaks at 72.3 and 75. 6 eV correspond to the Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2. The XPS analysis clearly confirms the formation of PdPt.

After comprehensive evaluation of PdPt nanostructure, we employed it for Suzuki cross coupling of organic halide and organic boronic acid to provide coupled diaromatics as presented in Fig. 5
(a). The experimental conditions were performed according to previously reported protocols [40] for the coupling reaction. A model reaction phenylboronic acid (122 mg, 1 mmol) and bromobenzene (100 mg, 1 mmol), and 2 M K2CO3 base (5 mL) are placed in a 25 mL RBF. The PEG-400 (10 mL) as an eco-friendly solvent and PdPt nanocomposite was used as a catalyst. The reaction mixture was heated for 4 h at 120 °C. Progress of the reaction was checked through TLC and continued until all starting materials were converted to biphenyl. The crude product thus obtained is filtered to remove the catalyst and extracted with water and toluene. The organic content was separated from filtrate and dried with sodium sulphate. The solvent was removed under reduced pressure. Finally, the crystallization of crude mass with ethanol resulted in pure biphenyl compound 118 mg with 94 % yield. The reactions were performed under identical conditions with varying PdPt catalyst quantities to identify optimal molar concentration (Fig. 5 b.) of the catalyst. The yield obtained for 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mol% of catalyst for the coupling reaction were 70, 82, 89, 94 and 94 % respectively. To understand the effect of various halogen atoms on the coupling reaction phenyl boronic acid is coupled with chlorobenzene and iodobenzene by using optimized experimental conditions (Table.1 ). However, yield of biaryl compound obtained in case of chlorobenzene is 61 % whereas the iodobenzene formed 95 % of the desired product. The reaction of 3-methoxy phenyl boronic acid with 3-bromo anisole resulted in the formation of 3,3′-dimehoxy biphenyl. The PdPt nanostructures were also utilized for the reaction of thiophene-2-boronic acid and 2-bromothiophene to obtain bithiophene with the yield of 94 %.

After completion of coupling reaction, the catalyst was removed by separation, cleansed with water and ethanol thrice, and dried for 2 h. The subsequent catalyst was used for subsequent reaction cycles up to 5 times. There is a loss of 7 % yield after 5 cycles as shown in Fig. 5 c, and further modifications to the catalyst and experimental conditions are essential to improve its effective exploration in coupling reaction. The XRD analysis of PdPt after 5 runs is illustrated in Fig S1 indicating the reduction of peak intensity is probably the main cause for the loss of yield. The cycle number and yield are also tabulated in Table S1.

In summary a simple one-step method for the synthesis of PdPt bimetallic nanoparticles has been developed via fenugreek derived polysaccharides and their catalytic behaviour is investigated through the Suzuki coupling reactions. The structure integrity of PdPt material is determined by the aid of analytical tools such as XRD, SEM, TEM and XPS studies. The PdPt nanoparticles have been employed in coupling reaction to obtain diaromatic compound. The catalyst was recycled for 5 repeated cycles demonstrating its potential as effective catalyst. This study provides a simple and feasible route to prepare PdPt and even other bimetallic nanoparticles with superior catalytic activities. This work does not only deliver an in-depth understanding into the rational originate and construction of bimetallic catalysts but also elucidate the vital role of exceptional nanostructures in coupling reaction in industrial applications. Further modifications to the catalyst and development of synthetic protocols by green approach are currently under progress in our laboratory to obtain the highest conversion of reactants into products.

Chinna Bathula: Writing – original draft, Methodology. K. Mallikarjuna: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Dhananjaya Rao Cuddapah: Formal analysis. K.N. Chidambara Kumar: Formal analysis. Hyun-Seok Kim: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Project administration. Cheolho Bai: Supervision. I. Neelakanta Reddy: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the research program of Dongguk University in 2023 (No. S-2023-G0001-00018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.112177.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
